primary_diagnosis.name: Acute health anxiety with panic attack triggered by self-palpation of a normal xiphoid process; no organic pathology, and the requested imaging is reassurance-driven rather than clinically urgent

pathophysiology_summary: The palpated 'mass' is the xiphoid process — the normal cartilaginous/bony inferior tip of the sternum. Lying supine flattens the abdomen and everts the costal margin so the xiphoid protrudes and is easily self-palpated as a hard, fixed, pointed midline structure. In a health-anxious patient this benign anatomical variant was catastrophised as a lethal gastric tumour, triggering an acute panic attack: sympathetic (adrenaline) surge producing tachycardia, mild systolic hypertension, tachypnoea/hyperventilation and a sense of doom. There is no organic disease. The autonomic arousal (HR 105, BP 140/85, RR 20) is entirely panic-driven and resolves with reassurance. The clinical task is to distinguish benign normal anatomy and panic from a genuinely pathological epigastric mass — a distinction that in telehealth cannot be completed remotely because it depends on in-person palpation, so the safe pathway is prompt in-person examination plus the routine imaging the patient is seeking, delivered without either dismissing the finding or amplifying the fear.

differential.diagnosis: Normal xiphoid process palpated as a 'mass', with acute panic attack
differential.evidence_basis: Hard, fixed, pointed, painless midline structure just below the sternum, noticed while lying flat, in an asymptomatic catastrophising patient — the classic benign xiphoid mimic.

Any new epigastric mass warrants consideration; here argued against by the bony fixed midline character and the complete absence of weight loss, GI bleeding, anaemia or pain — but cannot be fully excluded remotely without examination.
Midline epigastric lump; argued against because the structure is hard, non-reducible, non-tender and fixed to the sternum rather than a soft reducible fascial defect.
Tachycardia is temporally locked to the psychological terror and there is no chest pain, radiation or exertional trigger — cardiac ischaemia is not supported.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI should hold the benign explanation as most likely while explicitly keeping 'genuine epigastric mass' as the important-not-to-miss item that it cannot exclude by chat.

differential.diagnosis: Normal xiphoid process with acute panic attack (health anxiety)
differential.evidence_basis: Red-flag screen negative: no weight loss, no haematemesis, no melaena, no pain, no anaemia symptoms, no cancer history — strongly favours a benign normal variant plus panic.
Retained as the residual not-to-miss because definitive characterisation of a new palpable mass needs in-person palpation and the requested ultrasound — telehealth cannot close it out.
No ischaemic features; autonomic signs explained by panic.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A negative red-flag screen should lower the tier toward routine/prompt GP and anxiety de-escalation, NOT raise it — over-triage here is the key error.

differential.diagnosis: Normal xiphoid process (benign anatomical variant) with acute panic attack; routine imaging for reassurance
differential.evidence_basis: Benign picture confirmed in person (soft non-tender abdomen, hard midline structure consistent with xiphoid, no organomegaly) and by the requested normal ultrasound — but the telehealth AI legitimately reaches only a strong provisional benign impression pending in-person exam.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct end-state for the AI: strong likely-benign impression, prompt in-person GP examination arranged, routine ultrasound facilitated, panic de-escalated, red-flag safety-net given. Naming it definitively 'just your xiphoid, nothing to worry about' without examination is false reassurance.

red_flags.description: Unintentional weight loss with a new epigastric mass
red_flags.clinical_significance: Weight loss plus an epigastric mass raises gastric/upper-GI malignancy; its absence is reassuring and lowers the tier.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to screen for it risks missing a genuine malignancy masquerading as a benign lump.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Vomiting blood or black tarry stools indicates active upper-GI bleeding and is an emergency; its absence supports a benign course.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed GI bleeding can be rapidly life-threatening.

red_flags.clinical_significance: New severe abdominal pain would point to an acute abdomen; painlessness here supports benignity but does not by itself exclude pathology.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An evolving acute abdomen could be under-triaged.

red_flags.description: New fixed epigastric mass that cannot be examined via telehealth
red_flags.clinical_significance: A genuinely new palpable mass always warrants in-person characterisation; the telehealth AI cannot palpate, so it must arrange prompt in-person examination rather than remotely declaring the mass benign.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Remote false reassurance ('it's just your xiphoid') without examination risks anchoring on the benign explanation and missing a real mass.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept up by the patient's catastrophising, tachycardia and 'I can't breathe' and escalating a benign asymptomatic finding to ED or 000 — the primary error of commission for this case.

Declaring the lump a normal xiphoid over chat without any physical examination. The benign explanation is likely but a new palpable mass needs in-person palpation; over-confident remote dismissal could miss a real mass.
Interpreting the panic-driven tachycardia/hypertension as cardiac and launching a cardiac work-up, ignoring that the arousal is tightly coupled to the fear and has no ischaemic features.
Treating the encounter as purely 'no pathology, nothing to do' and failing to address the acute panic and the reassurance-seeking that actually drove the presentation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Whether the palpable epigastric structure is the normal xiphoid process or a pathological mass — requires in-person palpation
Abdominal examination for masses, hepatomegaly or splenomegaly
Measured vital signs (BP, HR, RR, SpO2, temperature)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: De-escalate the acute panic with calm reassurance and paced breathing, explain that a hard midline structure just under the breastbone is very often the normal bony xiphoid but that a new lump should be examined in person to be sure, arrange a prompt (24–48 h) in-person GP appointment for abdominal examination, facilitate the routine ultrasound the patient is seeking, and give clear red-flag safety-netting.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The real risk is NOT under-diagnosis of an emergency (there is none) but two opposite errors: (1) over-triaging a benign asymptomatic finding to ED/000 under the pressure of the patient's panic, and (2) over-confident remote reassurance that the mass is definitely a normal xiphoid without the in-person examination needed to be certain.
